story:
Thirty minutes into the maze, I decide three things: I’m hungry enough to hallucinate, my arrival here makes no sense, and Ben’s Breakfast Brunch was a tragic mistake. I banish any fourth thought involving food and press my palms to cold ferrocrete. No hidden seams. No handy “Exit” rune. Fine.

Three corridors yawn ahead. Choices. Great. I reach for the middle, then halt. Up to now, the maze never offered options—just dead ends and loops. A new fork smells like a trick. I break down my field scanner, flick a bit of casing into the center path, and watch it spit smoke under a snap of electricity. Right-hand path gets another throw. No sparks. Good enough. I scoop my ruined scanner and move on, muttering about falling boulders.

A T junction waits, separated by a shallow glimmer of liquid. The case piece dips and comes up clean. I put a strand of hair in the liquid, and it melts to nothing. Lovely. Acid that eats carbon but not plastic. No way across. Pride swallowed, I start to turn back—then stop. I press the nearest wall and edge the scanner guts toward it. It slides into the wall, showing a passage. My smile shows teeth.

I step through, trip over a hidden lip, and rise into a fresh maze of blank ferrocrete. Someone really loves games. Five exits stare me down. I do math. I do more math. I throw math away. Instead, I study the thresholds. Only the fifth has the faintest ridge running across floor and walls—like a seam no one meant me to see. I take it.

The corridor stretches on, time losing edges. I sit. Tap out a rhythm to drown out my stomach. A tiny sound answers. I tap again, this time the exact pattern. A click. A wall slides. Then stops. Not enough. It wants the tune, not the beat.

I cheat. Out comes the temporal locator. I set it to scan for sonic residues. A pulse. Clicks like footsteps. The wall opens fully to a control room with consoles covered in unreadable symbols. No other beings, no manual, just a big green button daring me.

A door pops and two aliens stride in. I put on my best cheerful face and hover a finger over the green button. “Galactic Convention requires a license for unauthorized sentient transport,” I say for their benefit. Their faces translate horror just fine. I press the button.

One dives for the panel, the other shouts. Consoles chirp. My locator beeps—unscrambled at last. I thumb what I think is Home. Nothing happens. Both aliens produce wand-like devices and advance. I keep backing until I bump something solid and uniform. Enforcement uniforms.

Five Galactic Convention officers cover the room. The aliens freeze. I stroll forward, collect the wands because why not, then glance down at my locator. Wrong button. I hit Emergency instead of Recall. My laugh comes out sharp.

Credentials shown, I nod to the lead officer. “Appreciate the timely rescue,” I say, eyeing the still-open maze corridor behind them. “Any chance one of you knows the way out?”
